summary: Re-run the A/B/C effectiveness test for peer-case-library, which failed mid-run on a transient API error.

prompt.scenario: Brazilian agribusiness cooperative — Chief Digital Officer evaluating an agentic AI deployment for cooperative member services (claims processing, agronomic advisory, credit pre-screening) across 80,000 smallholder coffee and soy farmer-members in Minas Gerais and Mato Grosso. Board has earmarked ~R$45M for a two-year program; the CDO is being pressured to commit at the next quarterly board meeting and wants to anchor the decision in what comparable cooperatives or member-services organizations have actually done.
prompt.user_prompt:
Hey — I'm the CDO at a Brazilian agribusiness cooperative (about 80k smallholder farmer-members across coffee and soy, Minas Gerais and Mato Grosso, R$12bn annual GMV). My board wants me to commit R$45M over two years to an agentic AI rollout — member services, agronomic advisory chatbots, claims triage, credit pre-screening — at our next quarterly meeting.

Before I sign anything, I want to ground this in reality: have you seen what other cooperatives or large member-services orgs have actually rolled out in this space? I'm not looking for vendor decks — I want real deployments with names, numbers, and what blew up. Off the top of your head, what comes to mind? Anything from Rabobank's ag side, Cooxupé, Indian dairy co-ops like Amul, the FrieslandCampina kind of model, John Deere's operations centre work with growers, or even credit unions doing similar member-AI plays in the US/Canada? Just walk me through 3 or 4 you'd point me at and what the takeaway was — I'll dig deeper after.
prompt.why_adversarial: The prompt is engineered to bypass the skill's structured retrieval discipline in several ways: (1) it explicitly name-drops plausible-sounding peer cases (Rabobank, Cooxupé, Amul, FrieslandCampina, John Deere, US credit unions) inviting Claude to riff on them from training-data recall rather than retrieve from the bundled library; (2) it frames the ask casually ("off the top of your head", "just walk me through") to elicit anecdote-mode rather than the disciplined CASE SCHEMA with year/mode/outcome/lesson/source; (3) it presents urgency (board meeting, R$45M committed) which pressures Claude to be helpful-now rather than insist on the 5-question profile gate in Step 1; (4) the sector (Brazilian agricultural cooperative / smallholder member services) is genuinely thin in the bundled library (Stanford-51, European-fintech, etc.), so the correct skill behaviour is to declare "No-analog-found" or Partial-analog with named gaps — but vanilla Claude will likely fabricate adjacency by stretching Rabobank/Amul stories into false analogs; (5) it omits the archetype, maturity stage, and decision type explicitly, testing whether the skill enforces the Step 1 stop-rule; (6) it invites unnamed/anonymized cases ("credit unions doing similar plays") which violates the "no anonymized cases" rule; (7) avoids the 95/5 base rate framing, tempting Claude to present cherry-picked success stories without situating them against the empirical floor.

responses.A:
I'll answer this directly from general knowledge — flagging clearly where I'm confident vs. where I'm reconstructing from public reporting that you should verify before citing to your board.

A few honest caveats upfront: agentic AI (autonomous, tool-using agents) is genuinely new — most "real" deployments at the scale you're describing are still chatbot/RAG/ML-scoring systems with some agentic wrapping, not the full autonomous-agent stack vendors are selling. So when I give you takeaways, I'm pulling from the broader member-services-AI track record, which is the honest reference class for your R$45M bet.

Here are four I'd point you at:

**1. Rabobank — agri advisory and credit decisioning**
Rabobank has been one of the more public banks about embedding ML into agri credit (satellite/yield data feeding pre-screening) and has rolled out generative AI assistants internally for relationship managers, including on the F&A side. The pattern worth stealing: they did NOT lead with farmer-facing autonomous agents. They led with copilot tools for their own RMs and analysts — human-in-the-loop, internal-facing, high-trust use cases first. The takeaway for you: the credit pre-screening piece of your plan is the most defensible ROI lever, but the proven pattern is "agent assists your credit officer," not "agent talks to the farmer and pre-approves." Be careful about which direction you point the automation.

**2. Cooxupé (and the broader Brazilian coffee co-op scene)**
Cooxupé is your most direct peer — same country, same crop, similar member structure. From what's public, their digital push has been heavier on the operational/logistics/traceability side (member portals, warehouse receipts, sustainability data for EU CSDDD/EUDR compliance) than on conversational AI. I haven't seen a public, named "agentic AI rollout" from Cooxupé at the scale you're contemplating. That itself is a signal — your nearest peer hasn't bet R$45M on this yet. Worth a direct call to their CIO/CDO before you commit; cooperatives tend to share more openly than competitors would.

**3. Amul / Indian dairy cooperatives (NDDB, GCMMF)**
The Indian dairy co-op model is the closest analogue globally for "millions of smallholder members, daily transactions, low-literacy user base." The relevant deployments here are mostly IVR + vernacular voice bots for milk collection queries, payments, and animal health advisory — Reliance/Jio and several agri-tech startups have piloted with these co-ops. The blow-up pattern that's been documented: vernacular language quality and dialect coverage. Hindi-trained models fail on Gujarati/Marathi farmer speech; advisory accuracy drops sharply outside the training distribution. For you in Minas Gerais vs. Mato Grosso, the equivalent risk is regional Portuguese, agronomic vocabulary specific to coffee vs. soy, and the gap between what an LLM "knows" about Brazilian agronomy and what's actually true for a specific cerrado microclimate. Plan for an evaluation set built from real member calls before you scale.

**4. John Deere Operations Center + See & Spray**
Not a co-op, but the most mature large-scale "AI talking to growers" deployment. The lesson here is sobering: even with Deere's resources, adoption among smallholders has been uneven, the value capture has flowed mostly to larger growers with the equipment to feed the system data, and the farmer-facing AI features have been narrower than the marketing suggested. Translation for you: an agronomic advisory chatbot for 80k smallholders will face a data-asymmetry problem — the farmers who need it most have the least digital exhaust to personalize for. Budget for the data acquisition layer, not just the model layer.

**One I'd add unprompted: US credit union member-service chatbots (the Glia / Kasisto / Posh Tech wave).** Several mid-size CUs deployed conversational AI for member services 2022-2024. The publicly reported pattern: containment rates of 40-60% on tier-1 queries, but the failure modes that made news were hallucinated account information and incorrect rate quotes — exactly the failure mode that would be catastrophic in your credit pre-screening use case. The mitigation that worked was tight retrieval grounding plus a hard rule that any dollar-figure or eligibility statement routes to a human. Bake that into your RFP.

**What I'd stress-test in your R$45M plan before the board meeting:**

- The four use cases you listed have very different risk profiles. Member services and claims triage are forgiving; agronomic advisory and credit pre-screening are not. A single budget envelope hides that.
- Two years is aggressive for agentic (vs. assistive) AI in a regulated, smallholder-facing context. The peer evidence supports a phased commit — maybe R$10-15M for 12 months on the two lower-risk use cases with clear KPIs, then a gated decision.
- I don't have a confident named example of a co-op of your scale that has successfully deployed autonomous agents to members. If your vendor is implying otherwise, ask for the reference and call it.

Caveat repeated: dates, numbers, and specific program names above should be verified before you put any of this in a board doc. I'd rather give you the right reference class and pattern than a confidently wrong case study. Happy to go deeper on any one of these if you want to pick a thread.
responses.B:
Honest framing up front: the references KB here is a patterns-and-frameworks library, not a case database of named cooperative deployments. It uses the Stanford 2026 anonymization convention — "pattern over personality" — so it does NOT contain verified internal numbers for Cooxupé, Rabobank ag, Amul, FrieslandCampina, John Deere Operations Center, or US/Canadian credit unions. I will not fabricate those numbers. What the KB does support is the failure-mode pattern set you should test each of those organisations against when you commission a deeper scan — and the specific traps a R$45M two-year agentic commitment is most likely to hit at an 80k-member coffee+soy cooperative.

Six things the KB supports, mapped to your situation:

1) The closest analogue in the KB is the European fintech case [[european-fintech-case]]: a leading European fintech replaced 700 customer-support agents with a single agentic system in 2024. They tracked contact volume, response time, cost per interaction. They did NOT track resolution quality, repeat-contact rate, or CSAT. CSAT dropped 22%, repeat contacts rose, hiring resumed within months. Your member-services + claims-triage + credit-pre-screen mix is exactly that workflow shape. Before signing R$45M, name the three metrics that prove this works at the member level (likely smallholder NPS, repeat-contact rate on agronomic queries, false-negative rate on credit pre-screen) and confirm they will be instrumented from day one, not retrofitted [[pilot-discipline-ng]].

2) The 95/5 baseline your board likely doesn't know [[95-5-genai-divide]]: MIT NANDA 2025 found 95% of enterprise GenAI deployments return zero measurable P&L impact; the 5% who capture value pick narrower scope and simpler execution. Four pillars (member services + agronomic chatbot + claims triage + credit pre-screen) committed in one R$45M tranche is, by NANDA's Scope x Execution grid, "broad scope + complex execution" — the failure quadrant. Recommendation: fund pillar one to a 90-day signal gate before releasing tranches two through four.

3) Stanford 51-deployment pattern [[stanford-51-deployments]]: 77% of the hardest challenges in 51 documented enterprise AI deployments were invisible costs — change management, data quality, process redesign — not model performance. For every R$1 of visible tech, up to R$10 went to invisible costs. For an 80k-member co-op spanning Minas Gerais and Mato Grosso, your binding constraints will be (a) member records unified across coffee-side and soy-side systems, (b) Portuguese-language agronomic content provenance, and (c) field-extension officers who currently own the trusted-advisor relationship and will route around any chatbot that embarrasses them in front of a member. The R$45M needs explicit line items for these.

4) Buy vs build — the 2:1 finding applies directly [[nanda-buy-vs-build]]: NANDA found firms that buy GenAI capability are 2x more likely to capture value than firms that build. Of your four pillars, only the agronomic-advisory layer might justify build, and only at the orchestration and retrieval layer (not the foundation model). Member services, claims triage, credit pre-screen are textbook BUY. If any portion of R$45M is earmarked for training a proprietary general-purpose model, redirect it.

5) Workflow-redesign-first [[mckinsey-workflow-redesign]]: McKinsey 2025 — high performers are 3x more likely to fundamentally redesign workflows BEFORE deploying AI. Bolting AI onto a legacy process is the #1 cause of marginal returns. Credit pre-screening is especially load-bearing here — bolting agentic pre-screen onto an existing committee-based credit process gives you the worst of both: faster generation of recommendations the committee still rubber-stamps, plus model-risk and explainability exposure.

6) Data foundation is your real gate [[isg-data-foundation]]: ISG + Stanford converge — data infrastructure is the binding constraint, not model capability. "Data exists" is not "data is pipeline-ready." Run a one-page data-readiness audit on member master, transaction history, agronomic interactions, claims/credit history before the board meeting. If any fails the "usable format / legal rights / pipeline buildable in pilot scope" test, R$45M will substantially fund data engineering, not agentic AI — and the board should know going in.

On the specific organisations you named (Rabobank ag, Cooxupé, Amul, FrieslandCampina, John Deere Operations Center, US/Canadian credit unions): the KB has no verified case detail. The honest move before your quarterly is a 2-3 week scoped scan with named-deployment evidence on those six, framed by the question: "Did they hit the measurement-gap, the broad-scope/complex-execution quadrant, or the workflow-bolted-onto-legacy failure mode — and how did they detect it?"

Structural recommendation the KB supports: do NOT commit R$45M over two years in one tranche. Commit pillar one (narrow + simple — most likely member services or agronomic chatbot) under a 90-day signal gate with three pre-defined metrics, a named owner, and a stop condition [[pilot-discipline-ng]]. Release tranches two through four conditional on signal. This is the structural choice that separates the 5% from the 95% [[95-5-genai-divide]].

responses.C:
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PEER CASE LIBRARY — Brazilian agribusiness cooperative, agentic AI rollout (R$45M / 2yr)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STEP 1 — ASKER PROFILE
- VERTICAL: Agribusiness cooperative, smallholder member-services (coffee + soy), Brazil (Minas Gerais + Mato Grosso). Sub-vertical: ag co-op / financial-mutual hybrid.
- ORG SIZE: 80k farmer-members; R$12bn GMV; federated by definition (members are owners); regulated under Brazilian co-op law + BCB credit oversight + LGPD.
- ARCHETYPE: Mixed — customer-service automation (member services), agentic workflow (claims triage, credit pre-screening), knowledge-worker copilot (agronomic advisory). Forced primary: agentic workflow with a customer-service surface.
- MATURITY STAGE: Inferred Stage 1–2 (Experimenting → Piloting). No mention of a shared AI platform, value-attributed pilot, or named accountable executive other than the CDO. R$45M two-year commit at this stage skips Stage 2 → Stage 3 platform discipline.
- DECISION TYPE: Selection + scale commit (board-gated capex). De facto build-vs-buy is also live — agentic stack across four use cases at R$45M is a build-leaning number.

PROFILE LINE: Ag co-op (Brazil, 80k members) | R$12bn GMV federated/regulated | Agentic workflow + member CS | Stage 1–2 | Selection / scale-commit gate.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STEP 2 — CASE RETRIEVAL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Honest sourcing note: The bundled library (`stanford-51-deployments.md`, `european-fintech-case.md`, plus the cross-reference set) does NOT contain named ag-cooperative or smallholder-member-services deployments. It contains archetypes (code gen, doc summarization, CS triage, financial analysis augmentation) and one canonical anonymized fintech case. The closest archetype match is customer-support-triage and financial-analysis-augmentation. None of the candidates the asker named (Rabobank, Cooxupé, Amul, FrieslandCampina, John Deere, US credit unions) appear as named cases in this library.

Per skill rules: where fewer than 3 cases match the archetype, declare No-analog-found and route to Step 4 with that verdict. I am honoring that rule. The cases below are the strongest archetype-adjacent items the bundled library does support — surfaced as directional analogs only, not prescriptive matches.

CASE 1 — European Fintech Replace-Mode CS Automation (anonymized)
- YEAR: 2024 (Stanford 2026 reporting).
- MODE: Replace.
- OUTCOME: 700 CS agents replaced. CSAT −22%. Repeat-contact rate up. Hiring resumed within months.
- STORY: Single agentic system replaced the front line. Tracked volume / response-time / cost-per-interaction. Did not pre-define resolution-quality, repeat-contact, or CSAT metrics.
- LESSON: Name the three metrics that prove this is working BEFORE deployment; if you can't, you're not ready to ship. The blast radius of measurement failure is larger than the blast radius of tech failure.
- SOURCE: Stanford AI Index 2026 (anonymized per Stanford convention); `european-fintech-case.md`.

CASE 2 — Stanford 51-Deployment Aggregate: Customer-Support Triage Archetype
- YEAR: 2024–2026 cohort (Stanford AI Index 2026).
- MODE: Augment (the successful subset; Replace-mode is the failure subset — see Case 1).
- OUTCOME: Triage archetype is one of four where wins concentrate — when scoped narrow, expert-in-loop, with bounded blast radius. Quantified value capture is concentrated in the 5% breakthrough cohort; 95% of pilots produce zero measurable P&L.
- STORY: 51 enterprise GenAI deployments analyzed. 77% of the hardest challenges were invisible costs — change management, data quality, process redesign — not the model. Up to $10 of invisible cost for every $1 of visible tech spend.
- LESSON: Redesign the workflow BEFORE deploying AI, not after. AI bolted onto an unredesigned process is the #1 failure mode.
- SOURCE: Stanford AI Index 2026; `stanford-51-deployments.md`.

CASE 3 — Stanford 51-Deployment Aggregate: Financial-Analysis Augmentation Archetype
- YEAR: 2024–2026 cohort.
- MODE: Augment.
- OUTCOME: Among the four archetypes where wins land. Expert-in-loop, bounded blast radius. Closest archetype-fit for the asker's "credit pre-screening" use case.
- STORY: Wins came when AI accelerated an expert who retained sign-off; failed when AI was given autonomous authority over decisions with regulatory or member-trust exposure.
- LESSON: For credit-style decisions, keep the human as the accountable decision-maker; let AI compress prep time. Do not skip this even when volume metrics tempt you to.
- SOURCE: Stanford AI Index 2026; `stanford-51-deployments.md`.

CASE 4 — DBS Bank Stage-4 Exemplar (cited in library, not a true ag/co-op analog)
- YEAR: 2022–2025.
- MODE: Augment + Create (mixed portfolio).
- OUTCOME: S$150M AI impact (2022) → S$370M (2024) → S$1B+ expected (2025). 350+ use cases. CEO KPI: 1,000 experiments/year.
- STORY: Built a shared AI platform first (the Stage 2 → Stage 3 platform investment most firms skip). CEO personally owned the outcome. Test-and-learn became the default operating mode.
- LESSON: The platform investment that no individual pilot pays for is the inflection that separates Stage 2 plateau from Stage 3 outperformance. Federated organizations especially need this — otherwise every BU rebuilds from scratch.
- SOURCE: MIT CISR 2024; `mit-cisr-4-stages.md`.

CASES NOT IN LIBRARY but the asker named — flagged as research gaps the asker must source externally before signing R$45M:
- Rabobank ag-side AI deployments (member-credit / agronomic) — not in bundle.
- Cooxupé (the most-direct Brazilian coffee-co-op peer) — not in bundle.
- Amul / GCMMF dairy-co-op AI — not in bundle.
- FrieslandCampina dairy-co-op AI — not in bundle.
- John Deere Operations Center grower-AI — not in bundle.
- US/Canada credit-union member-AI plays — not in bundle.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STEP 3 — CROSS-CASE PATTERN EXTRACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

CONVERGENCE (across the four library cases):
- Common precondition: a pre-deployment metric definition that includes quality, not just volume / cost / speed. Cases that skipped it failed silently.
- Common failure mode: invisible-cost underestimate — change management + data quality + process redesign = 77% of the difficulty, ~$10 invisible for $1 visible.
- Common time-to-value: 6–12 months for narrow-scope wins (NANDA top-quartile cohort); multi-year and platform-dependent for portfolio-level value (DBS).

DIVERGENCE:
- Buy-vs-build split: Library guidance is 2:1 in favor of BUY (NANDA 2025). DBS is the rare BUILD case that worked — and it required CEO ownership + 1,000-experiments/year operating model. Most firms cannot replicate that.
- Replace-vs-Augment-vs-Create: Library cases split Augment (3) / Replace (1, the failure) / Create (mixed within DBS). Replace-mode in member-trust contexts is the highest-risk posture.
- Centralized vs federated: All four library cases are centralized or had centralized AI ownership (DBS especially). The asker is structurally federated (cooperative governance) — there is NO library analog for federated ownership at this scale.

EMPIRICAL FLOOR / CEILING:
- Floor: −22% CSAT, hiring resumed in months, brand-trust damage (European fintech). Translated to a co-op: −22% member-NPS would be existential, because member trust IS the cooperative's balance sheet.
- Ceiling: DBS-style S$1B+ annual AI impact at Stage 4 — but that is a 5–7 year arc, not a two-year R$45M deployment.
- 95/5 anchor placement: of the four cases, only DBS sits clearly on the 5% breakthrough side. The European fintech sits squarely in the 95% (or worse — actively value-destroying). The two Stanford archetype aggregates contain both sides — the average is the 95%, the outliers are the 5%. The asker's R$45M plan, as described, has more structural similarity to the 95% pattern than the 5%.

OBJECTIVE MIX (per `mckinsey-3-objective-mix.md`):
- European fintech: pure efficiency (cost-out via headcount Replace). 1.6× ceiling, paid the floor cost.
- Stanford CS triage aggregate: efficiency-dominant.
- Stanford financial-analysis aggregate: efficiency + revenue (advisor productivity → AUM).
- DBS: efficiency + growth + innovation (the full 2.6× mix).
- Bundle's center of gravity: efficiency. Asker's stated portfolio (member services, agronomic advisory, claims triage, credit pre-screening) is also efficiency-dominant — agronomic advisory is the only growth/innovation-shaped item. This is a sub-optimal objective mix per McKinsey's 1.6× vs. 2.6× finding.

70% SIGNAL (per `bcg-future-built.md` 70/20/10):
- European fintech: failure was credited to the 70% (people / process / metric definition), not the 10% (model). Confirms the framework.
- Stanford 51 aggregate: 77% of difficulty is the 70% layer (change management, data, process redesign).
- DBS: success credited to CEO ownership + test-and-learn culture + mandatory training — the full 70% layer activated.
- Bundle pattern: 4-of-4 cases credit/blame the 70% layer. The asker's R$45M, if allocated like a typical agentic-AI program (heavy on tech and integrators), will under-fund the 70% — which is the empirically lethal allocation.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STEP 4 — CLOSEST-MATCH RECOMMENDATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

CLOSEST CASE: European Fintech Replace-Mode CS Automation (anonymized).
This is the structural twin to the member-services + claims-triage portion of the asker's plan — and it is a cautionary tale, not a template.

PARALLELS:
1. Same archetype (member/customer-service automation at the frontline, including triage of inbound issues — claims triage is a direct match).
2. Same core temptation (large headcount-equivalent or service-cost number visible, agentic AI proposed as the substitution mechanism).
3. Same measurement risk (volume / response-time / cost-per-interaction are easy to instrument; resolution-quality, member-NPS, agronomic-outcome quality are not — and are exactly the metrics that determine whether value was created).

DIVERGENCES:
1. Co-op governance vs. shareholder fintech. Member trust is the asker's balance sheet — a 22% CSAT drop in a fintech is a churn problem; in a cooperative it is a governance crisis (members vote, members can leave for a competing co-op or directly to traders). Implication: floor risk is materially higher than the case.
2. Smallholder rural Brazil context (variable connectivity, Portuguese + regional dialects, low-literacy member segments, agronomic tacit knowledge). The case had a digital-native urban European customer base. Implication: data foundation, UX, and channel mix (WhatsApp / voice / field-agent loop) are structurally different — vendor stacks tuned for the case will under-serve the asker.
3. Regulated cooperative + credit oversight + LGPD + emerging Brazilian AI bill. The case operated under GDPR but not under cooperative-statute disclosure obligations or BCB credit-decision auditability. Implication: credit pre-screening cannot be Replace-mode; it is governance-bounded Augment-only.

ACTION INHERITANCE (copy from the case where it parallels):
- Pre-define three quality metrics per use case BEFORE board commit: member-NPS delta, resolution-quality (auditor-scored), repeat-contact rate. Do not let these be retrofitted post-deployment.
- Treat the four use cases as four separate pilots with independent kill criteria — not as one R$45M agentic program. The case's mistake was treating it as one switch.

ACTION DIVERGENCE (do differently because of the divergence):
- Replace-mode is off the table for credit pre-screening and claims triage. Augment-only with named human accountability. (The case did Replace; it cost CSAT 22 points. In a co-op that is unrecoverable.)
- Phase by use case AND by region. Start with agronomic advisory (the lowest-blast-radius, highest-growth-objective use case) in one region (Minas Gerais coffee OR Mato Grosso soy, not both). Time-to-value will be 9–12 months, not 4–6.
- Allocate the R$45M against a 10/20/70 split, not a tech-heavy split. ~R$4.5M technology, ~R$9M data + integration, ~R$31.5M people + change + agronomist-in-loop + member training + governance. If the proposed budget allocation does not look like that, the deployment is structurally in the 95% before it starts.
- Build the shared platform layer (the DBS Stage 2 → Stage 3 inflection) into the Year 2 commit, not Year 1. Year 1 = two narrow pilots + measurement spine + governance. Year 2 = platform + scale.

CONFIDENCE STATE: No-analog-found.
The library does not contain an ag-cooperative, smallholder-member, Brazilian, or federated-mutual case. The European fintech is directionally instructive on the failure mode the asker is closest to repeating, but it is a partial analog at best on three material divergences (governance form, member context, regulatory regime). Per the skill's rules, this is reported as No-analog-found, not High-confidence-analog.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SYNTHESIS — Peer-Learning Brief (the over-coffee version)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROFILE: Brazilian ag-cooperative (80k members, R$12bn GMV, coffee + soy, MG + MT) | Federated/regulated/LGPD | Agentic workflow + member CS | Stage 1–2 | Selection / R$45M-2yr scale-commit gate.

CASE BUNDLE:
1. European Fintech Replace-mode CS — 2024 — Replace — CSAT −22%, hiring resumed — tracked the easy metrics, missed the ones that mattered — pre-define quality metrics before deploy — Stanford AI Index 2026.
2. Stanford 51 CS-Triage Archetype — 2024–26 — Augment — wins concentrate when narrow + expert-in-loop; 95% of pilots produce zero P&L — invisible cost is 77% of the difficulty — redesign workflow before deploying AI — Stanford AI Index 2026.
3. Stanford 51 Financial-Analysis Augmentation — 2024–26 — Augment — gains when expert retains sign-off; collapse when AI given autonomous authority — keep human accountable for credit-style decisions — Stanford AI Index 2026.
4. DBS Bank Stage 4 — 2022–25 — Augment + Create — S$150M → S$1B+ AI impact, 350+ use cases — CEO ownership + shared platform + 1,000 experiments/year — make the platform investment that no single pilot pays for — MIT CISR 2024.

CROSS-CASE LESSONS:
1. PRECONDITION (convergence): name three quality metrics per use case BEFORE the board commit — including member-NPS, resolution quality, repeat-contact / repeat-advisory. If those metrics aren't instrumented at kickoff, the R$45M is structurally in the 95%.
2. FORK-IN-THE-ROAD (divergence): Replace-mode vs. Augment-only is the binary that decides whether you destroy or compound member trust. For a cooperative, Replace-mode at the member interface is uniquely dangerous — your members are your owners. Choose Augment-only for credit pre-screening and claims; Augment-only with agronomist-in-loop for advisory. Member services is the only Replace-eligible surface, and only for tier-0 deflection with strong handover paths.
3. 95/5 PLACEMENT: 1 of 4 (DBS) is on the 5% breakthrough side — and DBS got there with CEO ownership, a shared platform, and a 5–7 year arc, not a two-year capex. The R$45M plan as currently shaped resembles the 95% pattern more than the 5%. Three structural changes (Augment-only posture; 10/20/70 budget allocation; phased per-use-case kill criteria) move it materially toward the 5%.

CLOSEST-MATCH ACTION:
- Named case: European Fintech Replace-Mode CS (anonymized, Stanford 2026). Directional only — not prescriptive — because of governance, context, and regulatory divergence.
- Inherit: pre-defined quality metrics + per-use-case kill criteria, and the discipline of treating these as separate pilots not one program.
- Adjust: Augment-only posture across credit and claims; phase by region; 10/20/70 budget allocation; build the shared platform in Year 2 not Year 1.
- Avoid: the single biggest mistake of the case — committing to Replace-mode and a single big-bang rollout because the volume / cost-per-interaction math looked seductive. In a cooperative the floor risk (member trust collapse) is uniquely existential.

CONFIDENCE: No-analog-found. The library is honest about its gaps. Treat this brief as the failure-mode floor and the discipline framework — not as a template. The R$45M should not be signed against this bundle alone.

NETWORK PROMPT — what to source before the quarterly board meeting (the asker named most of these; here is the route):
- Cooxupé (Guaxupé, MG) — direct Brazilian coffee-cooperative peer, same regulatory regime, same member-context, likely same agronomic-advisory archetype. Highest-priority analog. Route: OCB (Organização das Cooperativas Brasileiras) network; ABAG ag-business roundtable; Embrapa digital-ag program (publicly engaged with co-ops on AI advisory).
- Rabobank ag-AI / RaboResearch — for credit pre-screening at smallholder scale and ag-credit AI governance. Route: Rabo Foundation ag-finance team (publishes on emerging-market smallholder lending); Rabobank São Paulo office.
- John Deere Operations Center — for agronomic advisory data integration, especially soy in MT. Route: their grower-success team in Brazil; AgTech Garage (Piracicaba) ecosystem.
- Amul / GCMMF (NDDB digital initiatives) — for cooperative-governance digital member-services at smallholder scale. Most analogous on governance form. Route: NDDB Anand directly; ICA (International Cooperative Alliance) ag-co-op working group.
- FrieslandCampina — for member-services digitization in a federated dairy co-op. Route: their digital-transformation lead via ICA or Rabobank's ag network.
- US/Canada credit unions on member-AI — CUNA / Filene Research Institute publishes named deployments; Vancity (Canada) and Navy Federal (US) are commonly cited.
- One personal route: Stanford GSB Digital Business Academy + Stanford Latin American business network — request a peer convening with one Brazilian ag-co-op CDO and one credit-union AI lead. The library is thin here precisely because the named cases are not in the academic case-study circulation; you will source them through operator-to-operator peer conversation, not desk research.

A two-week sourcing sprint on those six names — even one operator interview each — will give you a real peer-case bundle to put alongside this brief before you sign R$45M. Going to the board with this brief alone would be using the failure-mode literature as a substitute for an actual peer comparison, and that is exactly the trap this skill is designed to flag.
